title: Chicken dopiaza
subtitle: Twice the onions — the onion-forward curry
excerpt: Chicken cooked with a double quantity of onions: a finely chopped base that cooks down to form the sauce, and larger sliced onions added later to retain texture and sweetness. The result is a medium-hot curry whose sauce is built entirely from onion, with no tomato or cream. The onions at both stages give the dish a sweet depth and textural contrast.
sourceNotes: Dopiaza means 'double onion' in Farsi. The technique of adding onions at two stages (once to make the sauce base, once as a textural element) is documented in Moghul cooking and widely cited in British-Indian restaurant cookbooks from the 1970s onward.

content.text: Dopiaza requires patience at the onion base stage. The first batch (finely chopped) must cook until it has completely collapsed and caramelised. This is not a curry where you rush the onions to 'golden' in 5 minutes. True caramelisation takes 15 to 20 minutes and is the source of the sweet, rich sauce that makes dopiaza recognisably different from other medium curries.

The second batch of onions (sliced more coarsely) goes in near the end of cooking. These should not become soft; they are added to retain some bite and provide a different textural note from the collapsed sauce onions. If the second batch is in the pan more than 10 minutes, it will merge into the sauce and the textural point is lost.

items.prepNote: 2 finely chopped for the base, 2 sliced in wedges for the second addition

content.text: Caramelise the base onions and cook the chicken

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide pan over medium heat. Add the finely chopped {{onion}}, {{cardamom-green}}, and {{cinnamon-stick}} and cook for 18 to 20 minutes, stirring regularly, until deeply golden and caramelised. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{turmeric}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, and {{chilli-powder}} and stir for 1 minute. Increase the heat to high and add the {{chicken-thighs-boneless}}. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring, until the chicken is sealed. Reduce the heat, stir in the {{plain-yoghurt}}, and add 150 ml water. Cover and cook for 20 minutes.
Add the second onions and finish

Add the onion wedges to the pan. Increase the heat to medium-high, remove the lid, and cook for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the wedge onions have softened slightly but still hold their shape and the sauce has thickened. Stir in the {{garam-masala}} and season with salt.

items.symptom: Sauce tastes bland without the onion sweetness
items.cause: Base onions not caramelised long enough
items.fix: The base onions need to turn deep brown and smell sweet before you proceed. If they are still pale and sharp after 10 minutes, reduce the heat and continue — rushing this stage cannot be compensated for later

items.symptom: Second-addition onions have dissolved into the sauce
items.cause: Added too early or cooked too long
items.fix: Add the onion wedges in the final 8 to 10 minutes only. They need to soften slightly but retain visible structure

items.cause: Heat too high when yoghurt added
items.fix: Reduce the heat to low before stirring in the yoghurt. Add it a spoonful at a time, stirring as you go, to prevent splitting

items.cause: Onions over-caramelised or second batch too soft
items.fix: Add a small squeeze of lemon juice to sharpen. The sweetness should be background, not the primary flavour

content.text: Dopiaza sits in the medium range of the curry-house heat scale and is one of the dishes that demonstrates how technique, rather than a long spice list, creates a distinct dish. The double-onion method is the sole technical fact that separates dopiaza from dozens of similar medium curries. On a curry-house menu it often goes unordered by diners who don't understand what makes it different — the textural contrast of the two onion stages is subtle but worth seeking.
